cases.question: Tell me about your work on the OCBC project at Deloitte.
cases.answer:
At OCBC, I was the sole backend engineer during the design phase, responsible for end-to-end architecture, workflows, API specifications, and detailed interface documentation for migrating the Personal Deposit System from .NET to Java microservices.

I developed the enquiry module using Kafka event-driven design and addressed unclear requirements by splitting sprints into a design preview phase and finalisation phase to secure earlier client feedback.

This reduced rework significantly and improved delivery predictability, enabling the team to proceed with external development partners while maintaining clear handoff documentation.

cases.question: How did you design the Kafka architecture at OCBC?
cases.answer:
At OCBC, I designed a single-topic event-driven architecture with unified event schema to keep the structure simple and extensible.

Different operations (Save, Withdraw, Renew, Enquiry) were distinguished by eventType field, while accountId served as the partition key to ensure per-account event ordering.

Each event carried a unique eventId for idempotency, and the API validated requests, persisted them as records with business data in JSON, then published to Kafka—with scheduled retry jobs ensuring at-least-once delivery and decoupling upstream and downstream services.

cases.question: What was your role and responsibility at OCBC?
cases.answer:
At OCBC, I was the sole backend engineer during the system design phase, responsible for end-to-end architecture, workflows, and API specifications.

I led a six-person delivery team through implementation, coordinating technical work, managing sprint priorities with the client product manager, and resolving blockers daily.

I personally developed the Enquiry module using Kafka event-driven design and ensured delivery stayed aligned with the original architecture.

cases.question: How did you handle unclear requirements at the OCBC project?
cases.answer:
At OCBC, I proposed splitting each sprint into a design preview phase and a finalisation phase.

This gave the client earlier feedback loops to clarify requirements before full development started.

The approach reduced rework and improved delivery predictability significantly.

cases.question: Tell me about your work at the Sanofi project.
cases.answer:
At Sanofi, I refactored a complex **10+** branch reverse-matching module buried in legacy code by systematically mapping business logic and building comprehensive unit tests.

I rewrote the entire test framework to use mocking and data-driven patterns, replacing brittle manual test setup.

The module became maintainable and testable; defects in that area dropped significantly after deployment.

cases.question: How did you handle the technical debt at Sanofi?
cases.answer:
At Sanofi, I took over the reverse-match module — a core feature with **10+** business branches, unclear requirements, and years of legacy code.

I reverse-engineered all logic branches using XMind and validated each one with stakeholders to create a complete specification.

I wrote comprehensive unit tests before refactoring anything, treating tests as executable specifications to preserve behavior.

I built the refactor in an isolated branch across multiple sprints to avoid disrupting ongoing delivery.

The refactor went live and significantly improved code maintainability and readability.

cases.question: Tell me about a production incident at Sanofi.
cases.answer:
At Sanofi, a null pointer exception appeared in production after I deployed a reverse-match refactor because legacy doctor records had missing fields that test and UAT environments never exposed.

I checked logs, identified the failure path within minutes, and joined a live call with the client to align on the fix strategy.

I created a hotfix branch, resolved the null-handling edge cases, validated in UAT, and deployed — the system stabilised immediately and has remained stable since.

cases.question: Tell me about your work at Alipay.
cases.answer:
At Alipay, I owned the design and delivery of a multi-environment deployment system that needed to run across isolated infrastructures with different dependencies.

I designed a single Maven multi-module codebase with profiles to dynamically activate environment-specific configurations, avoiding separate codebases that would have exceeded our **2-month** timeline.

The system successfully deployed to **100+ banks** and became the reusable pattern for other teams across the company.

cases.question: What was the biggest technical challenge at Alipay?
cases.answer:
At Alipay, deploying a financial data platform across 100+ isolated bank environments with different infrastructure in two months was the core challenge.

I redesigned the architecture using Maven multi-module with environment-specific profiles to enable single-codebase deployment instead of separate codebases.

The platform launched on time, achieved **60%+** department adoption, and improved developer efficiency by **over 20%**.

cases.question: How did you handle cross-environment deployment at Alipay?
cases.answer:
At Alipay, I designed a single codebase with Maven multi-module structure to manage different environment dependencies instead of maintaining separate codebases.

Maven profiles activated the right configuration at build time for each isolated environment—Alipay, government institutions, and banks with different infrastructure and networks.

I built a working demo first to validate the approach before rolling out to **100+** banks, achieving **60%+** department adoption.

cases.question: Tell me about your experience at Sinosig.
cases.answer:
At Sinosig, I designed and built a configurable policy processing module using Strategy and Factory patterns to digitize the entire insurance policy lifecycle from risk assessment to claims.

I resolved a recurring production crash occurring every **10** days by analyzing heap dumps, identifying unclosed HttpConnection objects causing memory leaks, and deploying a hotfix.

The system successfully processed credit insurance workflows without requiring code changes when business logic evolved, and the memory leak fix eliminated the crash cycle entirely.

cases.question: Tell me about the production issue you resolved at Sinosig.
cases.answer:
At Sinosig, the system crashed every **10** days due to unclosed HttpConnection objects causing memory leaks and OOM errors.

I analyzed heap dumps with MAT, identified missing close() calls in HTTP client code, and deployed a hotfix after leader approval.

The recurring outage stopped, and I established post-incident documentation practices to prevent similar resource management issues.

cases.question: Tell me about your experience at NetEase.
cases.answer:
At NetEase, I owned the activity submission system and participated in the promotion platform's pre-event stress testing and performance optimization.

I rewrote the submission detail page's critical interface by implementing batch queries and asynchronous calls to external services, reducing response time from **6s** to **300ms**.

The broader promotion system optimization improved throughput from **28k** to **320k+ QPS** through heap dump analysis, object structure refactoring, and JVM tuning over four months.

cases.question: Tell me about a time you disagreed with a team decision.
cases.answer:
At Alipay, the team planned separate codebases for 100+ bank environments, which I disagreed with given the two-month deadline.

I proposed a single codebase using Maven multi-module with profiles for environment-specific configuration and built a demo to validate it.

The platform deployed on time and was later adopted by **60%+** of departments for similar cross-environment deployments.

cases.question: Tell me about a time you delivered bad news to a client.
cases.answer:
At Alipay, the SDK team missed their delivery deadline and blocked our release.

I organised a cross-team meeting with both managers, clarified the dependency impact, and presented a phased delivery plan with clear ownership.

The client appreciated the transparency and the project shipped without further slippage.

cases.question: Tell me about a time you worked under tight time pressure.
cases.answer:
At Alipay, I designed and built a File Platform in two months with zero existing architecture and every bank requiring different infrastructure.

The original plan was separate codebases per bank, which I flagged as undeliverable in the timeframe. I proposed a single codebase using Maven multi-module with Spring profiles, built a demo, and got buy-in within days.

We delivered on time and the platform was adopted by 60%+ of departments, improving developer efficiency by over **20%**.

cases.question: Tell me about a time you proactively fixed a technical problem nobody asked you to.
cases.answer:
At Sanofi, a legacy reverse-match feature had complex unclear logic and was hard to maintain for years.

I proposed a refactor, mapped all business logic with the client, added full unit tests before changes, and ran it in a separate branch.

The refactor shipped without regressions and significantly improved maintainability for a long-standing pain point.

cases.question: Tell me about a time you had to learn something quickly.
cases.answer:
When I joined Alipay, I had to quickly master SofaBoot, an internal framework completely new to me.

I structured one week: read docs, set up local environment, build a multi-module demo with RPC calls, explore advanced features, pull the actual project, run and test it.

By week two I wrote production code successfully without ramp-up delays.

cases.question: Tell me about yourself.
cases.answer:
I am a Java and AI engineer with 7+ years building high-concurrency systems in banking, insurance, and e-commerce.

I have worked at NetEase, Sinosig, Alipay, and Deloitte, scaling systems to **320k QPS** and handling **TB-level** financial data.

My core skills are Java, Spring Boot, Kafka, Redis, MySQL, Docker, AWS, and Kubernetes — plus recent AI work with RAG systems.

cases.question: Why are you looking for a new opportunity?
cases.answer: I relocated to Australia and resolved visa constraints. Now I'm pursuing software development roles that leverage my Java backend and AI engineering experience.

cases.question: Have you ever worked in a startup environment?
cases.answer: I haven't worked in a startup, but Sinosig was similar—small **10**-person team building from scratch.

cases.question: What are your main strengths as a software engineer?
cases.answer:
At Alipay, I built and deployed a multi-environment file platform solo across 100+ bank systems in two months using Maven multi-module architecture.

I combined 7+ years of high-concurrency Java with production AI engineering—most engineers have depth in one, not both.

I've diagnosed and fixed real production incidents like OOM errors and JVM tuning issues under pressure, then restructured processes to prevent recurrence.

cases.question: What kind of role or team environment are you looking for?
cases.answer: Senior Java Engineer or Senior Backend Engineer role, ideally with AI/LLM or high-concurrency systems.

cases.question: How have you approached high-concurrency system design?
cases.answer:
At NetEase, I diagnosed a core API timeout under load by analyzing heap dumps with MAT and GC logs with JProfiler.

The analysis revealed that the promotion metadata object model was too heavy, triggering excessive Full GC cycles and promoting objects prematurely to the old generation.

I resolved it through object refactoring, batch query optimization, JVM tuning, and graceful degradation—scaling throughput from **28k** to **320k** QPS over four months.

cases.question: Why did you add a reranker to your RAG system?
cases.answer:
At Portfolio AI Knowledge Assistant, I added Cohere reranker because hybrid search alone couldn't always rank the most relevant chunk first.

Hybrid search (BM25 + dense vectors fused via RRF) optimizes for broad recall — it catches both keyword matches and semantic similarity. But ranking quality depends on how well BM25 and dense scores correlate, which they often don't.

Cohere rerank-v3.5 is a cross-encoder that re-scores the top **10** candidates from hybrid search using deep semantic understanding, then I take the top **5** for the LLM prompt. In roughly **30%** of queries, Cohere's top-1 differs from Qdrant's top-1 — meaning the reranker catches relevant chunks that the fusion algorithm ranked lower.

This reduced hallucination from irrelevant context and improved answer precision without requiring additional vector storage or retrieval infrastructure.

cases.question: How did you design for high availability at Alipay?
cases.answer:
At Alipay, the file platform relied on third-party SDKs without SLA guarantees, creating critical failure points.

I implemented rate limiting, circuit breaker logic, async fallback persistence, and scheduled retry with backoff to isolate failures.

The system achieved **100%** data integrity and remained functional even during complete SDK outages.

cases.question: How do you approach working with legacy code?
cases.answer:
At Deloitte, I reverse-engineered a **10+** branch legacy module by mapping business logic with the client and creating comprehensive unit tests before refactoring.

I isolated the work in a separate branch, completed the refactor over **2.5 months** using spare sprint capacity, and kept daily delivery uninterrupted.

The refactor went live smoothly and eliminated years of technical debt while preserving function integrity.

cases.question: Which project is the most challenging one you have ever participated in?
cases.answer:
At Alipay, I designed a cross-domain deployment architecture for **100+ isolated bank environments** under extreme two-month time pressure.

I proposed Maven multi-module with Maven profiles to build environment-specific packages from a single codebase, then convinced the team to adopt it over separate codebases through live demos and timeline analysis.

The system deployed successfully to internal and government systems, then scaled to **30+ banks monthly**, completing all **100+ bank deployments** within three months.

cases.question: What is your key contribution in the past few jobs?
cases.answer:
At Alipay, I built the File Platform for TB-level financial data across 100+ banks, solving cross-environment deployment under a 2-month deadline.

The platform was adopted by 60%+ of departments and improved developer efficiency by over 20%.

At Sanofi, I refactored a complex reverse-matching feature with 10+ branches by writing comprehensive unit tests first as executable specifications.

cases.question: How did you partition the Kafka topic?
cases.answer:
At OCBC, I used accountId as the partition key to ensure all events for a single account were processed in order.

This prevented race conditions where concurrent operations on the same account could be processed out of sequence.

The single topic with unified event schema and eventType field distinguished operations (Save, Withdraw, Renew, Enquiry) without topic explosion.

cases.question: What was the trickiest part of that refactor?
cases.answer:
At Sanofi, the reverse-match module had **10+ branches** with unclear logic — even the client couldn't fully explain some paths.

I mapped every branch visually using XMind and validated each one directly with stakeholders to confirm expected behavior.

Only after full clarity did I refactor, backed by comprehensive unit tests to ensure no logic changed during the rewrite.

cases.question: Which one taught you the most?
cases.answer:
At Sinosig, a production outage every **10** days taught me the most.

I debugged memory leaks in HttpConnection handling and added heap usage monitoring to catch issues before they exploded.

That experience locked in my commitment to observability and resource management as non-negotiable engineering practices.

cases.question: And the OCBC piece?
cases.answer:
At OCBC, I designed a single-topic Kafka architecture with unified event schema and eventType fields distinguishing operations.

AccountId served as partition key to ensure per-account event ordering, with unique eventId for idempotency.

The API validated requests, persisted them as records with JSON business data, then published to Kafka — scheduled retry jobs handled failures for at-least-once delivery.

cases.question: Have you used Kafka in production?
cases.answer:
At OCBC, I designed the event-driven Kafka infrastructure for the Personal Deposit System migration.

I architected a single-topic structure with unified event schema, using eventType to distinguish operations (Save, Withdraw, Renew, Enquiry) and accountId as partition key to ensure ordered processing.

The system achieved at-least-once delivery with scheduled retry jobs, decoupling **6** upstream and downstream services in the Hong Kong and Singapore deployment.

cases.question: What was your role at OCBC?
cases.answer:
At OCBC, I was the sole backend engineer during the system design phase, responsible for end-to-end architecture, workflows, and API specifications.

I spent six months redesigning the Personal Deposit System, producing architecture diagrams, sequence diagrams, detailed workflows, and comprehensive interface documentation.

The documentation was detailed enough that any developer could begin implementation without prior system or business knowledge, enabling the use of outsourced developers and reducing project costs.

cases.question: Tell me about Sanofi.
cases.answer:
At Sanofi, I owned the reverse-match module. This core feature had **10+** business branches, unclear requirements, and years of legacy code.

I refactored the entire logic systematically. I mapped all branches using diagrams, built comprehensive unit tests first, and kept work isolated to avoid disrupting delivery.

The refactor shipped successfully and significantly improved maintainability and stability.

cases.question: What about Netease?
cases.answer:
At NetEase, I was a backend team member on the Kaola promotion system handling **10+** promotion types at **320k QPS** peak traffic.

I participated in pre-event load testing, diagnosed Full GC bottlenecks via heap dump and GC log analysis, then optimized object structures and batch queries.

The core promotion APIs improved from **28k** to **320k QPS** over four months of iterative optimization.

cases.question: Describe Alipay project.
cases.answer:
At Alipay, I transformed a small internal data-transfer script into a production-grade platform handling TB-level financial data transfers across Alipay, government institutions, and **100+** banks.

The core challenge was deploying across **100+** isolated bank environments with different infrastructure and dependencies in only two months.

I designed a single codebase using Maven multi-module structure with profiles for environment-specific builds, delivered on time, and the approach was later adopted by other teams.
